metadata.title:
SEE
!. HANDWRITING

OLD METHOD OF WHEELING
OFFICIALS BY SMOOT n
AGENTS IS PASSING AWAY
IN THIS STATE At LAST.

pages.raw_text:
OLYMPIA, Feb. 3.-The passing
of the old order of railroad domin
alton in state affairs is happily IL
Illustrated in the desire of the
Northern Pacific to secure the
services Of State Tax Commission
er j. e. Frost to act as tax com
missioner for the road. And with
this effort on the part of the road
comes the announcement that Jay
Sedgewick of Tacoma, tax com
missioner for the Northern Pacific
ever since it entered the state,
will be retired.


For a generation Jay Sedgewick
has roamed about the state, calling
on county commissioners and
boards of equalization in the in
terest of the railroads taxation.
Knowing nothing of the theories of
taxation, he made men his study.
And he wheedled, cajoled, threat
ened and by the force of sheer
good fellowship and often vinous
sophistry, kept taxes at low fig
ure. He was hale fellow well met,
a funny story always bubbling and
his expense account was as elastic
as his duties might require. So
he went for years.


But now, good government and
the people have advanced to that
undreamed of stage when the rail-
roads are almost tickled to hys-
terics if they are able to get a
square deal in matters of taxation.
They have no use for good fell
lows" because the latter can no
longer deliver the goods.


Mr.. Frost. despite the apparent
gelidity of his name, is a good fell
low, but he does not make bust
ness of it. He knows taxes in
science, theory and art. He knows
assessable property, no matter ug-
der what alias or how disguised.


II the railroads fear that it will
not get a square deal in some coun-
ty, it contemplates having Mr..
Frost at hand with facts and fig
ures instead of wine and cigars,
to plead for them. Mr.. Frost will
do no hobnobbing, provide no ep-
tertainment; but appear like an
attorney before court, have his say
and go his way. :


And so the old order changeth.
